symbols: sower casting seed — the proclamation of God's Word to all who will hear
seeds scattering widely — the indiscriminate generosity of divine grace
birds in the distance — the evil one snatching the Word from those who do not understand
plowed earth — the human heart prepared or unprepared to receive the Word
dramatic sunset sky — the urgency of the kingdom message
description: A watercolor depicting a solitary figure striding across a plowed hillside, casting seeds with his right arm extended wide. He wears a blue-grey robe with a white headscarf or turban, and walks barefoot. His left hand holds a fold of his garment containing seeds. Small dark specks (birds) are visible in the middle distance. The sky behind is dramatic with golden-orange and grey tones suggesting sunset or dawn. The earth is brown and roughly tilled. The figure is rendered with dynamic, forward-leaning movement.
theological_description: Tissot's watercolor illustrates the Parable of the Sower, one of Jesus's most foundational parables, recorded in all three Synoptic Gospels (Matthew 13:1-23, Mark 4:1-20, Luke 8:4-15). Jesus described a farmer who went out to sow seed, which fell on four types of ground: the path (where birds ate it), rocky ground (where it sprang up quickly but withered), among thorns (where it was choked), and good soil (where it produced an abundant harvest). Jesus explained that the seed is the Word of God, and the soils represent different human responses. The sower's generous, wide-armed casting reflects the extravagance of God's grace — the Word is offered to all, not just to prepared hearts. As Isaiah declared: 'As the rain and the snow come down from heaven, and do not return to it without watering the earth... so is my word that goes out from my mouth: it will not return to me empty' (Isaiah 55:10-11). The birds visible in the distance correspond to Jesus's explanation: 'When anyone hears the message about the kingdom and does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown in their heart' (Matthew 13:19). The parable reveals that the Kingdom of God advances through proclamation, that reception depends on the condition of the heart, and that the harvest from good soil is superabundant — thirty, sixty, or a hundredfold.

scripture_refs.reason: The Parable of the Sower — the direct subject of the painting
Jesus's explanation of the parable — the seed is the Word and the soils are human hearts
Mark's parallel account of the Parable of the Sower
God's Word going forth and not returning empty — the prophetic foundation for the sower image
Luke's identification: 'The seed is the word of God'
